key_insight: The best solver for PDE-constrained inverse problems depends strongly on how the unknown is represented: grid-based parameters tend to favor adjoint optimization, while neural representations favor PINNs.
engineering_relevance: For inverse design, parameter identification, and constitutive modeling in solid and fluid mechanics, the paper clarifies when adjoints or PINNs are likely to be more accurate, cheaper, and easier to deploy.
method: Built a fair comparison from a common abstract formulation with identical domains, PDEs, observations, regularization, optimizer, parameterization, and precision.
Benchmarked unsteady Burgers, noisy Darcy permeability inversion, 3D Allen--Cahn reaction identification, and unsteady Navier--Stokes viscosity identification.
Tested a PINN-warm-started adjoint hybrid to combine PINN initialization with adjoint-level refinement.
evidence_signal: Across four benchmark inverse problems, the study compares reconstruction quality and cost under matched settings and reports that time-dependent cases can make adjoint methods expensive due to trajectory storage and differentiation.
watch_item: Performance depends on the chosen unknown parameterization and time-dependent adjoint implementations can be memory-intensive, so fairness and scalability need careful setup.
paper_title: Adjoint Method versus Physics-Informed Neural Networks in PDE-Constrained Inverse Problems
